content.problemStatement:
Alice has NNN magical balls placed on the xxx-axis. The iii-th ball is initially at position XiX_iXi​ and has a power of PiP_iPi​.

It is guaranteed that Xi<Xi+1X_i \lt X_{i+1}Xi​<Xi+1​ for 1≤i<N1 \leq i \lt N1≤i<N.

Initially, none of the balls are activated.

Alice can do the following **at most twice**:


Choose an index iii (1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N) such that ball iii is not activated.
Activate ball iii, and choose a direction for it (either right or left).
When ball iii is activated in a certain direction, the following process takes place:


The ball will move PiP_iPi​ units in the chosen direction.
If, while moving, it strikes another ball jjj, ball iii will immediately disappear. Then,

If ball jjj was previously activated, nothing happens, and the process immediately ends.
Otherwise, ball jjj activates in the same direction as ball iii (that is, if ball iii was moving left, ball jjj will move left too).

Note that the power of ball jjj doesn't change, and remains PjP_jPj​.


If it never strikes another ball, it simply stops after moving a distance of PiP_iPi​ units.

In this case, the ball does not disappear.
Using at most two moves, is it possible for Alice to activate every ball?

Note that Alice can make her second move only after the entire process from the first move has completed.

See the samples below for an explained example.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of balls with Alice has.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers X1,X2,…,XNX_1, X_2, \ldots, X_NX1​,X2​,…,XN​ — the positions of the balls.
The third line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ — the powers of the balls.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `YES` if Alice can ensure that at every ball has been activated in the end, and `No` otherwise.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `NO`, `No`, `nO`, and `no` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: Xi<Xi+1X_i \lt X_{i+1}Xi​<Xi+1​ for every 1≤i<N1 \leq i \lt N1≤i<N.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: No matter what Alice does, it's impossible to activate all five balls.
Test case 222: Alice can do the following:

Activate ball 222 towards the left.

It will move P2=5P_2 = 5P2​=5 units to the left. On its way, it will hit ball 111 at position X1=1X_1 = 1X1​=1.
This causes ball 222 to disappear, and ball 111 to activate towards the left. Ball 111 will move P1=1P_1 = 1P1​=1 unit and then stop, not hitting anything.


On her second move, activate ball 333 to the right.

This will cause it to move P3=3P_3 = 3P3​=3 units, and hence hit ball 444.
Ball 333 disappears, and ball 444 will activate and move right.


All four balls were activated at least once, as desired.

Test case 333: No matter what Alice does, it's impossible to activate all four balls.
Test case 444: Alice can first activate the first ball rightwards, which will hit the second ball and activate it. The second ball will move rightward one unit, and the process ends.
Then, Alice can activate the fifth ball leftwards. That causes the following steps:

The fifth ball will hit the fourth. The fifth ball disappears, and the fourth activates to the left.
The fourth ball will hit the third. The fourth disappears, and the third activates to the left.

All five balls were activated, so the answer is YES.
Test case 555: Alice can activate the first and third balls rightwards, and they'll hit (and hence activate) the second and fourth balls, respectively.
